sentences.text: Can you stop in the middle of the 30 minutes?
My name is Klara Samuels.
K-L-A-R-A S-A-M-U-E-L-S.
Salamon, S-A-L-A-M-O-N.
Well, I have used a different name for a while during the war.
Do you want me to tell you?
Barbara, B-A-R-B-A-R-A, Lozinska, L-O-Z-I-N-S-K-A.
Yes, I did.
Yes.
My name was Nielsen for a brief time, N-I-E-L-S-E-N.
You know, not now, but when I was a child I had a number of different nicknames.
I could not pronounce the word Klara, so they called me Tala.
That stopped after I grew up.
October 26, 1927.
Well, that makes me 68.
Still.
[LAUGHS]
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
Yes.
My father was a doctor in Warsaw.
He specialized in bacteriology.
And his name was Dr.
Mojzesz Salamon.
And my mother, her name was Roza Salamon, born Salamon.
She had the kind of life that every Jewish American princess dreams of.
She did nothing.
She had two servants.
She spent her morning talking to her friends and deciding what we would have for dinner.
And she spent her afternoon trying on clothes, and getting tickets to concerts, and playing the piano.
She had the coloratura soprano voice, but neither her father, nor my father-- her husband-- encouraged her to, to perform publicly, so she just performed for friends.
I had a very happy childhood.
I went to school.
It was not a public school, but it was in Polish, not, not in Yiddish.
The name of the school-- I will tell you, but no one can pronounce it.
Zjednoczenie Nauczycieli.
Do you want me to spell it?
Z-J-E-D-N-O-C-Z-E-N-I-E, that's the first word.
Then the second one is N-A-U-C-Z-Y-C-I-E-L-I.
And after all of that, this means Teachers Union.
Well, I was in grammar school.
When the war started, I had just completed sixth grades, so I studied the usual things-- the history, and the Polish language, arithmetic, a little science, mostly sort of biology, botany, and so on.
We had-- the school owned some land in a park in Warsaw.
And during the spring, we, the children, would have our plots, and we would sow some plants, and then grow some vegetables and flowers.
Once or twice a week during the spring, we would travel to this location and have our classes outdoors.
Now that I think of it, it was really a pretty good school.
My father-- that, that did not exist in Poland.
You know, this is sort of an American invention.
There was no PTA.
My parents attended-- I was interested in acting as a child, and I was in several plays in school.
And, of course, my parents would come and see the plays.
I also-- I wrote poetry-- terrible poetry, of course, but my mother was very proud of me.
I would be unable not to show it to her because I loved it, and I wanted her to see it.
And I would always make her promise me she wouldn't show it to anybody, and she always invariably broke the promise.
She was proud of me, of course.
You know, when you have your own children, you understand your parents' actions much better.
But a really carefree life.
We went away every summer for the whole summer.
Well, we went to different resorts in Poland to get the fresh air, as my mother would put it.
And there were no camps, really.
That was an unknown concept.
And the last coup-- the last couple of years-- oh, my father would join us usually for a couple of weeks.
And when he came, we would do all the sightseeing.
I'm really-- my interests were always much closer to my father.
He was very interested in my education throughout my life, really, and encouraged me always.
He was a great believer in education-- a very conservative man, so conservative I don't know if you will even know what it means.
But he was a follower of Jabotinsky.
Jabotinsky was the man who, who thought up the ideas of Irgun during, you know, the armed resistance, and so on.
Very-- politically, very conservative, and so was my father when he was here.
He, he thought that Goldwater was a socialist, you know?
But at the same time, everybody has incongruencies in their lives.
My father believed in education for women, and so he always encouraged me to learn.
And he would supervise.
He would try to find books for me to read when I was a child.
And when we were on vacation, when he came we would do sightseeing and all sorts of things that he thought were interesting and educational.
Yeah.
No.
No, I don't.
And it was all in Polish.
I-- you know, I did have some poetry that I wrote during the war.
And it's somewhere in the house.
I don't know where, but I once showed it to my husband, and he told me I was a terrible poet.
So he didn't spare me.
[LAUGHS]
Well, a couple of things were written in English.
That's why he could tell me.
Because I did speak English already, as I'll tell you later.
No, no, no, no.
It's this husband.
Yeah, this husband.
This, this husband-- I've been married for over 40 years.
So the other marriage is like a dream.
It's like it never happened, really.
You know, I think my father would have liked to have a kosher home.
I know he did because after the war, he asked me to keep a kosher home.
My mother was dead by that time.
And I was so unused to it.
I really was reluctant, and I didn't do it.
We did not have a kosher home.
We belonged to what is now known as the Great Synagogue of Warsaw, located on Tlomackie Street.
And I remember going there.
We always went there for the first day of school because the school I went to was actually a Jewish school, even though they conducted the lessons in Polish.
We learned some Hebrew, and we had the Jewish religion as one of the subjects.
And the first day of school, we went to the temple, I think, to be all blessed for the coming year and so on.
And I think I remember going once or twice on the high holidays.
We had seders.
We celebrated Purim in the house.
But not, for example, lighting candles every Friday night.
And so it was probably what you would call here a kind of a reformed household with some minimum of observance.
Oh, I don't know if they even had such distinctions.
I don't remember it as a child.
Probably, it was sort of like conservative, but I don't even remember.
I think the men and women sat together, which was probably would be considered very reformed at that time in Poland.
My father's background was a very religious background.
Yiddish was the language that they had spoken, apparently, at home.
And he went to cheder, you know?
Then, of course, he went to a Polish gymnasium.
That's high school.
And then was educated in Konigsberg and eventually in Kharkiv.
There's an interesting story about that.

When my father came to United States, which was in late 60's from Israel, he got his license to practice medicine here because United States had a reciprocity treaty with Imperial Russia on licensing doctors, which expired in 1914.
My father's degree was from 1912 from Kharkiv.
And on that clause, he got into-- he got his license to practice in the United States.
It's a grandfather's clause.
Yes.
Of course, I speak Polish.
I speak some Russian, and I speak pretty good French.
I understand a lot of Yiddish.
I speak a little bit of German.
And I used to speak Hebrew fluently, but I forgot it almost completely.
I, I can follow a service a little bit in a temple and read, but I don't understand half of the words.
Well, I learned French-- I had a French governess when I was a little girl, which was the sort of thing that the Polish intelligentsia-- Jewish intelligentsia in Poland, people learned French.
This was the lingua franca of Europe at the time.
And I learned Russian during the war.
I went-- this goes already into the war years, but I attended Russian school for about a year and a half, and I learned Russian.
My father-- both my parents spoke Russian.
My mother had been born in Minsk in Russia, in Belarus.
And my father helped me by reading with me Eugene Onegin in Russian.
And then I learned Hebrew when I was in Israel after the war.
I learned German-- don't ask, out of thin air.
And the same with the little bit of Yiddish.
When I was a child, as I told you, I wrote poetry.
I loved to tell stories.
I would collect with some of my girlfriends, you know, and I would make up highly romantic, you know, stories about how it would be when we grew up.
In my opinion at the time, sometimes off color, but very naive, naturally.
I was a little girl.
And I loved reading.
I always loved reading, and I do to this time.
I played a lot.
I played with dolls.
I think I was a very imaginative little girl because even I remember playing with dolls, it was not just dressing and undressing.
But I would always imagine myself on a deserted island, you know, or all kinds of unlikely situations, and make up stories about it.
I had some very good girlfriends.
And we were close.
We went to parks.
We played.
As I told you, we traveled during the summer.
I was twice abroad.
And it just will show you how progressive my father was.
In our house, all the ideas came from my father.
I was once in France in 1937 during an international exposition in Paris and twice in then Czechoslovakia.
And I remember that my father insisted that we start going-- I start going to the opera.
And so I had seen two operas before the war-- hard, difficult operas, Faust and Eugene Onegin-- poor choices for first operas for a child.
Yeah, so I did a lot of different things as a child.
I am very uninterested in sports.
When I was teaching here in United States, it took me seven years to find out where the football field was at the high school.
But I did swim.
I skated.
I skied as a child.
But, you know, it was not really engaging seriously in sports.
We went every winter for two or three weeks to a winter resort-- which I'm going to visit when I will be in Poland in June of this year.
And that's the sort of thing that we all did there.
We would ski and skate during the day.
And then the children-- and I was-- I was always a big operator as a child.
I would arrange for us to put on a performance towards the end of our stay of different skits, and, and songs, and all kinds of little things.
And we would-- we would charge for attending this performance.
Of course, all the parents came.
And then I remember that on the last day, we would go to a Jewish orphanage to whom we donated the money that we had collected.
And because I was the big organizer and producer of this affair, I always went, you know, to this.
And really, when I think about it, now I kind of cringe that they would take this child, introduce me to this whole class of children.
And probably was rather humiliating for them to have me come up.
And I brought the money and so on.
[LAUGHS] Not, not the best-- not psychologically the most clever situation.
But that's-- i think it inculcated in us the importance of sharing what we had with other people.
The school that I went to, for example, you had to bring two lunches-- one for you and one for another school, which was like a sister school where the children were poor.
And we provided the lunches for them.
To my knowledge.
I do-- my father knew, of course, a lot of people who were not Jewish, but we lived in a Jewish part of Warsaw.
You know, before the war there was, of course, no ghetto.
But people do tend to flock-- particularly when, when you live in a country which was anti-Semitic.
I didn't sense it at all.
And I think my parents protected me from it by sending me to a private school so that I would not come in contact with children who were taught anti-Semitism and so on.
So all my friends-- my contact with, with Gentiles was always through the help that we had.
We always had-- we had a cook and a maid.
The maids were originally from the country.
We had one maid who when, when she was shown how to flush a toilet, she thought there was a demon in there, you know?
But my parents would bring these maids from the country and then sort of civilize them.
And that was really my contact.
I remember one maid who was very religious and who tried to make me into a Catholic and took me to the nearby church.
And I remember she introduced me to the priest.
And the priest knew me as the little Jewish girl who wanted to be a Catholic.
But then, of course, she left and I-- [LAUGHS]
No, but you know, I liked her, so I went along with it.
I was-- I was a kind of a goody-goody little girl, but not really.
I was often mischievous.
I remember, we used to do telephone jokes.
Call people and tell them, for example, is this the zoo?
And they would say no.
And say, how come an ape came to the phone, you know, or a gorilla came to the phone?
You know, this kind of children's jokes.
And but I-- you know, I remember that I was always the initiator of all such things, so--
Yes.
We were well off.
My father, I told you, was a doctor.
Oh, his patients came from all different levels of society.
Mostly, they were Jews, but there were some non-Jews.
For example, I remember that at one point during the war, we met a Polish man who turned out to have been my father's patient.
But he worked-- there was no Medicaid in Poland, so most doctors had private patients, paying patients, and the rates would differ depending on the ability to pay.
And they also did a lot of pro bono work, and almost pro bono work through clinics, et cetera, and so my father did the same kind of things, all kinds.
He specialized in bacteriology.
He had the laboratory right in the apartment where we lived.
And he performed all the different analysis, different tests.
His avocation, though, was research.
He was always trying to make something out of something else.
And if he didn't need to make a living, I think he would have been kind of a basic research person-- which by the way, interestingly enough, my oldest son is.
He's a molecular biologist and likes to do basic research.
Well, more in my father's, really, than mine.
Well, my life changed very abruptly at 4 o'clock in the morning on September 1, 1939 when the first bombs fell on Warsaw.
I-- there were, of course, intimations of the coming troubles, naturally, in Europe.
And I, I knew, being 11 and a 1/2-- I didn't really understand it.
As a child, I really was in many ways, much more naive then an 11 and a 1/2 year old would be in United States.
I even remember being a little excited about the coming-- possible coming of the war, absolutely not realizing the implications of it.
I knew that the, the grownups were very worried.
We were brought back.
We came back from our vacation a little early.
And everybody was hoarding food, buying a lot of food, and laying in supplies, and all kinds of things.
But when it's-- really, when the bombing started, you know, our first thought was that it was an air raid test, which we had had in the last few days.
But, of course, it turned out to be the real thing.
As you probably know, Warsaw was besieged for months, so the shock was total and immediate.
We were bombed day and night because within several days, Warsaw was surrounded.
So we were bombed by planes during the day and by artillery at night.
The building where we lived was an apartment house because Warsaw is a city.
And most of the bombs, by the way, were incendiary type.
And our building was bombed.
There were some fires, and we had to escape.
Maybe it was around September 10 or so.
And I remember that we ran out of the building.
The streets were strewn with corpses.
And as we were running, my mother fell on a body, and I helped her to get up.
And I just remember the horror of it.
We ran to a house of some friends.
And we all huddled in a hallway where there were no windows because the windows were broken, and the glass was dangerous.
And I remember sitting there-- I'm sure we did other things, but that's my memory, sitting there and praying.
I don't think I was ever as religious again as I was during those 20 days, praying constantly for our lives.
There was no electricity, no gas, no water.
People had to go to the river to bring some buckets of water.
And food was limited, but there was some.
And at the end of that-- but I also remember soldiers in the streets, passing through Warsaw and the wounded people.
Then at the end of the month, of course, Warsaw was occupied by the Germans.
Well, at first they were Polish retreating, and then they were German soldiers.
At the beginning, there were tremendous shortages immediately.
We never went back to our apartment, but I remember we were staying with an uncle and aunt of mine.
Their names were Hurwicz, Zina and Adek.
Z-I-N-A and A-D-E-K H-U-R-W-I-C-Z.
And the, the grownups had decided-- even though the violence at the beginning was sporadic and sort of random, there was not yet a concerted effort against Jews.
Of course, we knew already from the wars-- the years before the war what the German attitude was towards Jews.
Well, you know, before the war, what the Germans did with the Jews was they killed some, but they exiled many.
And so there were refugees.
As a matter of fact, I remember that one of my girlfriend's, her parents took in a refugee family from Germany.
So that we knew that the Germans-- we'd read in newspapers and so on-- it was known.
Although the Final Solution was not envisaged.
It could not be imagined by anybody, Hitler's Final Solution for the Jews.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we did know.
And my parents had decided that they would try to escape to Wilno, which was a town taken over at the time by Lithuania.
Should I explain how it happened?
Certainly.
Yeah.
OK.
When the war started, as you know, there had been a nonaggression treaty between Germany and Russia.
And around the middle of September, while Hitler was occupying the Western part of Poland, Russia came into the eastern part and ceded a part of Poland-- the northeastern corner-- to Lithuania.
It had always been a kind of disputed territory.
And Wilno, the biggest city-- which I believe is now the capital, as a matter of fact, of Lithuania-- was taken over by Lithuania.
From Lithuania, it was possible to escape from Poland.
And in fact, one of my uncles and one of my aunts made it, but we didn't.
